brief.problem: AI agents lose project context after a task or session ends, so long-running work becomes fragmented across tools and time.
brief.user: Target user not yet fully defined — see What To Do Next.
brief.matters: The claimed pain is repeated context loss between January and February work, especially when different agents touch the same project.
brief.next:
- Talk to 5 builders who use more than one AI tool on the same project
- Find 2 current memory/work-history workarounds and identify what they miss
- Write down the exact moment context loss breaks a workflow
- Test whether users would connect sensitive work history to a shared memory layer

assumption_map.assumption_label: Builders using multiple AI agents repeatedly lose useful project context between sessions and tools.
assumption_map.user_evidence_quote: Most agents "forget" context once a task is done.

assumption_map.assumption_label: The first target user is a builder or operator running long-lived projects across Claude, GPT, Cursor, and custom agents.
Users will trust a secure, user-owned shared context layer with sensitive work history.
Existing agent tools expose enough integration surface for cross-app memory to be useful.
The market lacks an open, interoperable memory layer despite movement toward personalized AI.
assumption_map.user_evidence_quote: Google, Microsoft, and Anthropic are all racing toward “personalized AI,” but none have cracked open, interoperable memory yet.

eval_scorecard.reason: The pain is concrete and plausible, but the current evidence is still founder-claimed rather than observed.
eval_scorecard.failure_mode: The product may solve a real annoyance that users tolerate rather than an urgent workflow blocker.
eval_scorecard.improvement_tip: Collect recent user stories where forgotten context caused measurable rework, delay, or bad output.

eval_scorecard.reason: The idea names broad users and tools, but no specific person or narrow first segment.
eval_scorecard.failure_mode: The target user could become everyone who uses AI, which makes discovery and positioning blurry.
eval_scorecard.improvement_tip: Pick one first segment, such as AI-native solo builders managing client projects across Cursor and ChatGPT.

eval_scorecard.reason: The architecture is plausible, but the first integration wedge is not proven.
eval_scorecard.failure_mode: Universal infrastructure may be overbuilt before one useful workflow works.
eval_scorecard.improvement_tip: Validate one two-tool memory loop before expanding the infrastructure.

eval_scorecard.reason: Existing incumbents are mentioned, but actual user workarounds are not yet described.
eval_scorecard.failure_mode: Users may already solve enough of this with project docs, tool-native memory, or manual summaries.
eval_scorecard.improvement_tip: Document current workarounds before proposing a vector database or shared context layer.

eval_scorecard.reason: Most critical assumptions are missing or claimed, not backed by observed user behavior.
eval_scorecard.failure_mode: The idea can sound strategically inevitable while remaining unvalidated at the user level.
eval_scorecard.improvement_tip: Run discovery before architecture: users, trust boundaries, current workflows, and willingness to connect data.

decision_brief.one_sentence_idea: Build a secure, user-owned shared context layer that lets AI agents across apps remember long-running project history.
decision_brief.target_user: Not yet specific enough; likely AI-native builders or operators using multiple AI tools on long-running projects.
decision_brief.pain_chain: Agents forget prior work, users re-explain context, outputs degrade or repeat work, and projects lose continuity across time and tools.
decision_brief.current_workaround: Not yet validated; likely manual project docs, copy-pasted summaries, tool-native memory, or ad hoc notes.
decision_brief.riskiest_assumption: Users will trust and adopt a cross-app memory layer for sensitive project history.
decision_brief.evidence_needed: Observed examples from real multi-tool AI users showing repeated context loss, current workaround failure, and willingness to connect work history.
decision_brief.invalidation_threshold: If fewer than 3 of 10 target users report weekly context-loss pain or none would connect project history to a shared layer, revise or park the idea.

next_3_experiments.assumption_tested: Multi-tool AI users repeatedly lose important project context.
next_3_experiments.method: Interview 10 builders/operators and ask for their last 3 AI-assisted project workflows.
next_3_experiments.success_signal: At least 6 describe recent context-loss rework without being prompted.

next_3_experiments.method: Ask 5 users to screenshare how they preserve context between AI tools today.
next_3_experiments.success_signal: At least 3 show brittle manual workarounds with clear failure points.

next_3_experiments.assumption_tested: Users will grant a shared layer access to useful project context.
next_3_experiments.method: Show a permission mockup and ask users what data they would allow, deny, redact, or delete.
next_3_experiments.success_signal: At least 5 of 10 accept a scoped permission model for one real workflow.

honest_gaps: No named first user segment yet.
No quantified frequency or cost yet.

calibration.rule_applied: If any axis score is 1 or Evidence score is <= 2, decision must be PARK or PIVOT. If all axes are >= 3 and Pain is >= 4, decision may be PROCEED TO PROTOTYPE.
